title: Recovering Troubled Projects
summary: How to spot a project sliding towards failure and lead a disciplined, honest turnaround that salvages value. In 8th-Edition terms, recovery lives in the Risk and Scope performance domains: diagnose before acting, then protect the value that can still be realised.
intro: Diagnose, stabilise and re-plan - turn a red project around without panic.

sections.heading: How recovery maps to the 8th Edition
sections.body: A turnaround is not a special process group - it is the Risk and Scope performance domains under pressure. The move that scores is always the same: diagnose before acting, and measure success as value protected from here, not the original baseline.

blocks.title: Principles at work
lines: Risk domain: surface the real status and attack the biggest threats first.
Scope domain: salvage the scope that still delivers value.
Be an Accountable Leader + Stakeholders: tell the truth early to rebuild trust.
Focus on Value: judge recovery by benefit still realisable, not sunk effort.

blocks.title: One rule to carry
blocks.text: Diagnose before you act. You cannot re-plan credibly on top of a status you do not trust.

sections.heading: Warning signs and how to read them
sections.body: Trouble rarely announces itself. It shows up as a pattern of small signals that, taken together, say the project is drifting. Lean on objective trends to cut through the optimism.

groups.label: Response
lines: Stop and establish the real status before promising new dates.

items.title: Budget burning faster than work is done

groups.label: Response
lines: Check CPI; re-plan spend from current reality, not the plan.

groups.label: Response
lines: Freeze low-value churn; re-baseline through change control.

items.title: Morale sinking or best people leaving

groups.label: Response
lines: Protect and re-motivate the team before adding pressure.

groups.label: Response
lines: Treat as a leading indicator; find the root cause, not the symptom.

items.title: Status reports go quiet, vague or stay 'green'

groups.label: Response
lines: Dig into how status is measured - reporting no longer matches reality.

blocks.title: Objective trends beat optimism
lines: CPI or SPI under 1 = behind on cost or schedule.
Watch how fast schedule or budget buffers are consumed.
Buffer burn surfaces trouble before a deadline is formally missed.

blocks.title: The revealing cue
blocks.text: A report that is always green is itself a red flag. When reporting stops matching reality, you have a trust problem on top of a delivery problem.

sections.heading: Common root causes
sections.body: Most troubled projects fail for a handful of familiar reasons rather than one dramatic event. Name the real causes honestly - fixing symptoms while the causes remain just buys a short reprieve.

groups.label: Cause
lines: Requirements were ambiguous or keep shifting.
The team is forever building against a moving target.

groups.label: Cause
lines: Optimistic estimates and schedules compressed to hit a date.
No room for the normal friction of delivery.

groups.label: Cause
lines: Light up-front planning.
Risks identified but never actively managed.
Predictable problems become surprises.

groups.label: Cause
lines: Sponsor absent, disengaged, or keeps changing 'done'.
Decisions stall; the project cannot self-correct.

blocks.title: Diagnose honestly
blocks.text: Name the real root causes - underneath many failures sits a sponsorship problem. Without a steady owner, nothing self-corrects.

sections.heading: What recovery actually aims for
sections.body: Recovery is not the same as the original definition of success. The original plan promised a certain scope by a certain date for a certain cost, and that promise is usually already broken. Chasing it anyway tends to deepen the hole.

blocks.title: Reset the goal
lines: Goal = minimise total failure and salvage as much value as possible from here.
That may mean reduced scope, a later date, or delivering only the part that still makes business sense.
Start by resetting expectations honestly with sponsors and stakeholders.
Tell them what is genuinely achievable now - not a re-run of the comforting numbers that got the project into trouble.

blocks.title: Measure from today
blocks.text: Measure recovery against value salvaged from today, not against a baseline that is already gone.

sections.heading: The recovery process
sections.body: Work the turnaround in clear stages rather than flailing at the loudest problem: assess -> stabilise -> re-plan -> communicate.

items.title: Assess the real status
lines: Get an unvarnished picture before committing to anything.
Confirm what is actually complete versus reported.

items.title: Stabilise - stop the bleeding
lines: Pause low-value churn.
Establish a status you can trust before re-planning on top of it.

items.title: Re-plan from current reality
lines: Rebuild the plan from where the project truly is.
Ground estimates and risks in what has actually been learned, not the original wishful figures.

items.title: Communicate and decide at a gate
lines: Hold an explicit go / continue-with-changes / kill decision before more money goes in.
Funding the next phase is a deliberate choice, not the default.

blocks.title: Order matters
blocks.text: Stabilise first - you cannot re-plan credibly on top of a status you do not trust.

sections.heading: Leading the recovery through people
sections.body: A recovery is led as much through people as through plans. Bad news delivered early and clearly rebuilds the credibility that the troubled phase eroded.

items.title: Communicate transparently
lines: Be open with the sponsor and stakeholders.
Deliver bad news early to rebuild trust.

items.title: Re-baseline formally
lines: Put reset scope, schedule or budget through proper change control.
New targets are agreed and visible, not informally assumed.

items.title: Protect and re-motivate the team
lines: People are often demoralised and stretched.
Burning them out guarantees a second failure.

items.title: Sequence work by risk
lines: Tackle the biggest threats and most uncertain assumptions first.
Learn early whether the recovery is viable while you still have options.

blocks.title: Attack risk first
blocks.text: Attack the largest risks first - they decide whether recovery is even possible.

sections.heading: Sunk-cost discipline
sections.body: The hardest judgement in a recovery is deciding whether to continue at all. Money and effort already spent are gone whatever you choose next, so they should not drive the decision.

blocks.title: Decide on future value
lines: Weigh the cost and risk of finishing from here against the benefit still available.
A project can have consumed a fortune and still be the right thing to cancel today.
Killing a doomed project early is a success, not a defeat.
It frees people, money and attention for work that can actually pay off.

blocks.title: Frame the question right
blocks.text: Ask what finishing is worth from here - never what has already been spent. Failing cheap beats failing expensively after pouring in more.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam and re-tailored to 8th-Edition performance domains.
